coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent probe based on AuNCs induced AIE for quantification and visual sensing of glucose
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Xiaoli
Wu, Peiling
Gu, Mengying
Xue, Jian
coredata.dc:description: Blood glucose measurement plays a very important role in clinical diagnosis and fluorescence analysis has attracted extensive attention. A novel ratiometric fluorescent system with aggregation induced emission (AIE) property for the detection of glucose was established in this work. In this system, bovine serum albumin-stabilized Au nanoclusters (BSA-AuNCs) served both as the fluorescent detection probe and the AIE inducer. An AIE molecule, named sodium 1,2-bis [4-(3-sulfonatopropoxyl) phenyl]-1,2-diphenylethene (BSPOTPE), served as fluorescent reference probe. In the presence of H2O2, the fluorescence intensity of BSPOTPE/BSA-Au NCs at 680 nm progressively decreased while that at 490 nm remained constant. Glucose can be catalyzed by glucose oxidase (GOx) and produces H2O2. Therefore, glucose detection can be conveniently achieved by the proposed strategy. The fluorescence intensity change ratio increased linearly with the glucose concentration in the range 1–8 mM. Moreover, the proposed method exhibits a consecutive fluorescence color change (“from red to cyan”) to glucose concentration in the range of 1–8 mM under a 365 nm UV lamp and exhibits bright “red” or bright “cyan” in lower glucose concentrations (lower than 3 mM) or high glucose concentrations (higher than 7 mM), respectively. The work offers an ideal rapid clinical diagnostic method for both normal and abnormal blood glucose screening.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent probe
AIE
H2O2
Glucose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs ratiometric fluorescent probe
Detection of glucose in human serum samples
BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs for ratiometric detection of H2O2
BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs for quantification and visual screening of glucose
Selective determination of prepared glucose biosensor
Detection of glucose in human blood serum samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of BSA-AuNCs. (B) XPS spectra of Au 4f of BSA-AuNCs. (C) Zeta potential of BSA-AuNCs and BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs. (D)The fluorescence spectra of BSPOTPE (25 μL, 0.1 mg L−1) with different concentrations of BSA-AuNCs (From bottom to top: 0, 5, 10, 25, 50 μL and 50 μL of BSA-AuNCs without BSPOTPE). Insert: the image of a 365 nm UV lamp.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs in the presence of different concentrations (0–8 mM) of H2O2. (B)Plot of the relationship between (I0–I)/I0 and H2O2 concentration (0–1 mM and 1–8 mM).
(A) Fluorescence spectra of BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs in the presence of different concentrations (1–8 mM) of glucose. (B)Plot of the relationship between (I0–I)/I0 and glucose concentration (1–8 mM).

The evolution of fluorescence color of BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs in presence of different concentrations of glucose in the range of 1–8 mM under a 365 nm UV lamp. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The selectivity of the BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs ratiometric fluorescence probe to various coexisting species (the concentration of glucose is 5 mM and other coexisting species are 0.5 mM).
Schematic illustration of the BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs ratiometric fluorescence probe for sensing of glucose.
Comparison of the proposed methods with various methods for glucose detection.
Results of glucose determination in serum samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The glucose determination was performed in the hospital for clinical analysis.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent probe based on AuNCs induced AIE for quantification and visual sensing of glucose

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Medicinal Plant Development, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences & Peking Union Medical College, Beijing, 100193, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences & Peking Union Medical College

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Medicinal Plant Development, Chinese Academy of Medical Science & Peking Union Medical College, Beijing, 100193, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Blood glucose measurement plays a very important role in clinical diagnosis and fluorescence analysis has attracted extensive attention. A novel ratiometric fluorescent system with aggregation induced emission (AIE) property for the detection of glucose was established in this work. In this system, bovine serum albumin-stabilized Au nanoclusters (BSA-AuNCs) served both as the fluorescent detection probe and the AIE inducer. An AIE molecule, named sodium 1,2-bis [4-(3-sulfonatopropoxyl) phenyl]-1,2-diphenylethene (BSPOTPE), served as fluorescent reference probe. In the presence of H2O2, the fluorescence intensity of BSPOTPE/BSA-Au NCs at 680 nm progressively decreased while that at 490 nm remained constant. Glucose can be catalyzed by glucose oxidase (GOx) and produces H2O2. Therefore, glucose detection can be conveniently achieved by the proposed strategy. The fluorescence intensity change ratio increased linearly with the glucose concentration in the range 1–8 mM. Moreover, the proposed method exhibits a consecutive fluorescence color change (“from red to cyan”) to glucose concentration in the range of 1–8 mM under a 365 nm UV lamp and exhibits bright “red” or bright “cyan” in lower glucose concentrations (lower than 3 mM) or high glucose concentrations (higher than 7 mM), respectively. The work offers an ideal rapid clinical diagnostic method for both normal and abnormal blood glucose screening.

ce:section.ce:para: Glucose is the most abundant monosaccharide, which plays a significant role in metabolic homeostasis by providing energy in living systems to maintain human functioning [1]. The normal range of blood glucose concentrations is about 3.9–7.8 mM (2 h after food) or 3.9–6.2 mM (empty stomach) [2]. Blood glucose level is seen as a sign of human health status, and the abnormal concentrations of blood sugar suggest the presence of many disease or an increased chance of developing diseases such as diabetes or hypoglycemia [3,4]. Therefore, the monitoring of blood glucose level is of great significance to clinical diagnosis. To date, electrochemical [5,6], fluorescence [7], and absorptiometry [8] have been extensively developed for glucose monitoring. Compared with the well-developed electrochemical approach, fluorescence methods have attracted extensive attention owing to their high sensitivity, good stability, low cost, and test rapidity [9,10]. Ratiometric fluorescent method, which contains two or more emission bands at different wavelengths, has better adaptability with strong anti-interference capacity than the single-wavelength fluorescent probe. Similar to internal reference approach used in many other analytical methods, ratiometric fluorescence method is much reliable [2,11,12]. Based on a hybrid of red-emitting and green-emitting CdTe QDs, Zhou’s group provided a visual and fluorescent method for detection of glucose [13]. Using cationic conjugated polymer and CdTe/CdS QDs as ratiometric fluorescent probe, Huang’s group demonstrated a near-infrared ratiometric fluorescent method for determination of glucose [14].

However, the conventional fluorophores, such as semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) mentioned above, emit bright fluorescence in dilute solution but emit weak fluorescence due to profound self-quenching in the aggregated state or at high concentration [15]. This phenomenon of aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) is very common and resulting in low signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) and poor photostability [16]. Aggregation induced emission (AIE), which was introduced by Tang group in 2001 [17], is a unique photophysical phenomenon that some molecules are nonemissive in dilute solution but the fluorescence greatly enhanced in aggregated state. Because of their high photostability and good portability in solid state, AIE fluorogens (AIEgens) has caused widespread concern since its discovery. AIEgens have been applied in various areas such as biomolecular sensing, biological imaging, chemical sensing, and optoelectronic systems [15,16,18]. Tetraphenylethene (TPE) and its derivative are widely used fluorophores for designing probes. For example, a TPE derivative named sodium 1,2-bis [4-(3-sulfonatopropoxyl)phenyl]-1,2-diphenylethene (BSPOTPE) has been reported for sensing of human serum albumin (HSA) [19], bovine serum albumin (BSA) [20], histone deacetylase [21], glutathione [22], biomolecule [23], acetylcholinesterase activity and organophosphorus pesticides [24]. Besides, based on BSPOTPE-SiO2–MnO2 sandwich nanocomposites, our group established a fluorescence sensor for the detection of organophosphorus [25].

In this work, a novel ratiometric fluorescent system based on BSA-stabilized Au nanoclusters (BSA-AuNCs) induced AIE of BSPOTPE was constructed for the determination of glucose and the principle is depicted in Scheme 1 . BSPOTPE is nonemissive in the absence of BSA-AuNCs but becomes highly luminescent in the emission band of 400–550 nm in the presence of BSA-AuNCs. Therefore, the BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs fluorescent probe shows two emission bands between 400 and 550 nm (peaking at about 490 nm) and 550–800 nm (peaking at about 680 nm), respectively. In the presence of H2O2, the fluorescence intensity of BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs at 550–800 nm progressively decreased due to H2O2-induced oxidation of AuNCs [26]. It is well-known that glucose can be catalyzed by glucose oxidase (GOx) and produces its hydrolysates and H2O2. Therefore, glucose detection can be conveniently achieved by the proposed strategy. According to the relationship between the fluorescent intensity ratio I (I680/I490) change and the glucose concentration, a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe for the quantification of glucose was developed. Moreover, based on the color changes of ratiometric fluorescent system in presence of different concentrations of glucose under a 365 nm UV lamp, a rapid clinical diagnostic method for glucose screening was developed.

HAuCl4·3H2O (≥99.9%), GOx from Aspergillus niger (100 U mg−1), analytical grade dopamine, ascorbic acid, acetaminophen, uric acid and biotechnology grade albumin from bovine serum (BSA, >98%) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., LTD. Maltose, fructose, sucrose, glucose, and xylose were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other analytical reagents were purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing).

Fluorescence spectra were recorded by a TECAN Infinite M1000 PRO multifunctional microplate reader (Tecan Group Ltd., Switzerland). Zeta potentials were obtained via a Nano Is Malvern Zetasizer (UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected on a Kratos AXIS X-ray photoelectron spectrometer and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired on JEM-7500 TEM (JEOL, Japan).
BSPOTPE was synthesized according to the reported procedure by derivation of TPE [25,27] and the detailed information was provided in supporting information.

BSA-AuNCs were synthesized according to the literature [28]. Firstly, 5 mL of HAuCl4 solution (10 mM) and 5 mL of BSA solution (50 mg mL−1) were mixed and stirred vigorously at 37 °C. Then 0.5 mL of NaOH solution (1 M) was added and the mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 12 h to obtain a homogeneous deep brown solution. The solution was diluted 10 times and kept at 4 °C for further using.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs ratiometric fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs ratiometric fluorescent system was prepared by simply mixing the BSPOTPE solution and BSA-AuNCs solution. Typically, 25 μL of BSPOTPE (100 mg L−1) and 25 μL of BSA-AuNCs were incubated at 37 °C for 10 min. The BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs complex was diluted with phosphate buffer (PB, 10 mM, pH 7.0) to 500 μL for further characterization. The fluorescence spectra of the system was recorded and fluorescence intensity at 490 nm and 680 nm was recorded as I490 and I680, respectively. The fluorescence intensity ratio I680/I490 is recorded as I.

(a) Sensing of H
2
O
2 . 50 μL of BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs complex, 50 μL of H2O2 solution with different concentrations, and 100 μL of phosphate buffer (PB, 10 mM, pH 7.0) were mixed and diluted with deionized water to 500 μL. After incubating at 37 °C for 30 min, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture was recorded. (b) Sensing of glucose. 50 μL of BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs complex, 100 μL of GOx (500 μg mL−1), 100 μL of glucose solution with certain concentrations (1–8 mM), and 100 μL of phosphate buffer (PB, 10 mM, pH 7.0) was mixed and diluted with deionized water to 500 μL. After incubating at 37 °C for another 30 min, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture was recorded. (c) Quantification of the target analyte (H
2
O
2
or glucose). Quantification of the target analyte was calculated according to the relationship between the fluorescence intensity change ratio (I0–I)/I0 and the target analyte concentration. Where I0 and I is the ratio I680/I490 in the absence and in the presence of the target analyte, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: 50 μL of BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs complex, 100 μL of GOx (500 μg mL−1), 100 μL of phosphate buffer (PB, 10 mM, pH 7.0), and 100 μL of human serum sample was mixed and diluted with deionized water to 500 μL. After incubating at 37 °C for another 30 min, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture was recorded.

The morphology and size of BSA-AuNCs were characterized by TEM (Fig. 1 A). TEM image shows that AuNCs display a quasi-spherical shape and have a diameter of about 2 nm. XPS of BSA-AuNCs (Fig. 1B) displays the binding energy of Au 4f7/2 could be deconvoluted into two distinct components centered at Au+ (87.56 and 84.15 eV) and Au0 (87.05 and 83.48 eV), which are in good agreement with previously reported AuNCs [29,30].

Fig. 1C displays that zeta potential of BSA-AuNCs and BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs is −38.3 mV and −49.5 mV, respectively. The results indicate that both BSA-AuNCs and BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs are negatively charged. It has been known that BSPOTPE is negatively charged due to the two sulfonic acid groups in the molecule [25]. Therefore, BSA-AuNCs and BSPOTPE are with like charge and the BSA-AuNCs induced AIE of BSPOTPE is not attributed to the electrostatic attraction.

Fig. 1D illustrates the fluorescence spectra of the BSA-AuNCs, BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs complex, and BSPOTPE excited at 365 nm, respectively. It shows that when tested alone, BSPOTPE possesses weak fluorescence and BSA-AuNCs show an emission band from 550 nm to 800 nm (peaking at about 680 nm). The BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs complex, however, show two fluorescence emission band of 400–550 nm (peaking at about 490 nm) and 550–800 nm (peaking at about 680 nm), respectively. Indicating that effective AIE of BSPOTPE occurs in the presence of BSA-AuNCs. When keeping the amount of BSPOTPE constantly at 25 μL (0.1 mg L−1), and gradually increasing the amount of BSA-AuNCs from 0 μL to 50 μL, the fluorescent intensity of BSPOTPE increased firstly (from 0 μL to 10 μL) and remain unchanged (from 10 μL to 50 μL). Besides, with the increase of BSA-AuNCs, the mixture shows color changing of green-yellow-orange-red under a 365 nm UV lamp (the insert image in Fig. 1D). It has been known that restriction of intramolecular rotation is the main reason of AIE and the binding of BSA can lead to the induced fluorescence of AIE molecules [20]. Therefore, BSA reduced AuNCs can also induce fluorescence of BSPOTPE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs for ratiometric detection of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the quenching effect of H2O2 on AuNCs through H2O2-induced oxidation, AuNCs has been applied for sensing of H2O2 [26]. In this study, the dual emission BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs fluorescence probe was studied for ratiometric detection of H2O2. The fluorescence spectra of BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs in the presence of variable H2O2 concentrations (0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 8 mM) was record and the corresponding fluorescence intensity change ratio (I0–I)/I0 was calculated (Fig. 2 ). Fig. 2A shows that as the H2O2 concentration increased, the fluorescence emission band at 680 nm progressively decreased and that at 490 nm remained constant. It indicates that BSA-AuNCs are sensitive to H2O2 as previously reported [26], while BSPOTPE is insensitive to H2O2. The fluorescence intensity change ratio (I0–I)/I0 values were calculated over the H2O2 concentration range of 0–8 mM (Fig. 2B). The dependence of (I0–I)/I0 on H2O2 concentration follows two equations in two concentration ranges: (I0–I)/I0 = 1.7822 × 10−7+ 0.6063 × [H2O2] (r = 0.9980) from H2O2 concentration of 0 mM–1 mM and (I0–I)/I0 = 0.5348 + 0.0322 × [H2O2] (r = 0.9951) from H2O2 concentration of 1 mM–8 mM, with LOD of 0.1 mM (The LOD was calculated by the equation LOD = (3σ/s), where s is the slope of the calibration curve and σ is the standard deviation of blank signals), respectively. The phenomenon of two linear range was often observed in nano sensors and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [31–35]. Such results might result from the integration of the enzymatic reaction as well as the H2O2 induced quenching course but its exact cause is not clear [32,34,35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs for quantification and visual screening of glucose
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows that the BSPOTPE/BSA-AuNCs exhibit a consecutive fluorescence color change (“from red to cyan”) to glucose concentration in the range of 1–8 mM under a 365 nm UV lamp. In the concentration range of 3–8 mM, the samples exhibit a color change from light “red” and light “cyan”. While under low glucose concentrations (lower than 3 mM) or high glucose concentrations (higher than 7 mM), the system exhibit bright “red” or bright “cyan”, respectively. Because the normal range of glucose in healthy human blood serum is about 3.9–6.2 mM (empty stomach), this proposed visual method offers a rapid clinical diagnostic method for glucose screening.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective determination of prepared glucose biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To test the selectivity of this proposed method for glucose determination in human blood serum, the interference substance which may coexist in human blood serum was examined. The fluorescence intensity change ratio (I0–I)/I0 of the samples was calculated in the presence of other glucose analogs (including xylose, maltose, fructose, and sucrose) and some coexisting metal ions (such as Na+, K+, Mg2+, and Ca2+). Besides, the interference of some common small molecules in human serum such as dopamine (DA), ascorbic acid (AA), acetaminophen (AP), and uric acid (UA) was also investigated. The results (Fig. 5 ) show that obviously decreased fluorescence response for glucose is distinguished from the other species, which indicates that the proposed method has high sensitivity for the determination of glucose in human blood serum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose in human blood serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To check the practical applicability of the proposed method, three blood serum samples from healthy human (the concentrations of glucose were in the range of 4–6 mM) were tested and spike-and-recovery experience (the spike level of glucose was 2 mM) was conducted with the proposed method. The results (shown in Table 2 ) illustrate that the glucose amount estimated by the proposed method is generally well consistent with reported levels. Furthermore, the recoveries of the added glucose in human serum were in the range of 97.0–104.1% and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were below 5%. All the tests indicated that our proposed method did have good applicability for glucose detection in the real blood samples.

This study introduced a ratiometric fluorescent method for sensing of glucose based on BSA-AuNCs introduced AIE. According to the relationship between the fluorescent intensity ratio change and the glucose concentration, a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe for the quantification of glucose was developed. The quantification range of glucose is 1–8 mM, which is generally well consistent with the concentrations of glucose in healthy human blood. Moreover, the proposed method exhibits a consecutive fluorescence color change (“from red to cyan”) to glucose concentration in the range of 1–8 mM under a 365 nm UV lamp and the system exhibit bright “red” or bright “cyan” in low glucose concentrations (lower than 3 mM) or high glucose concentrations (higher than 7 mM), respectively. Therefore, the proposed ratiometric fluorescent method not only has good precision for normal blood glucose detection but also has facile and visual advantages for abnormal blood glucose detection. This work provides an ideal approach for designing ratiometric fluorescent probe with AIE property, which offers a rapid clinical diagnostic method for glucose screening.

Xiaoli Wu: Methodology, Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Peiling Wu: Resources, Investigation. Mengying Gu: Resources, Investigation. Jian Xue: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was founded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81803721), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 3332018098), and National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2018YFC1602102).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2020.01.004.

Pr. 93 (2011) 179-186.
